---
title: "STAT 479: HW3"
author: Spring 2026
format: pdf
execute:
    echo: true
    message: false
    warning: false
    cache: false
---

# Submission Details

Upload your solution to Gradescope as a PDF document. Include all relevant
figures within your explanations. Include all relevant code. You may use either
R or python. You may review this assignment's source code [here](https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/blob/master/exercises/hw3.qmd).

## Evaluation Criteria

**Technical accuracy**: Definitions used correctly, claims stated precisely.

**Completeness**: Assertions grounded in readings, lectures, or original code.

**Clarity**: Well-formatted, grammatically correct prose (not bullet fragments). No wordiness.

**Code quality**: Modular functions, meaningful variable names.

```{r}
#| label: libraries
#| echo: false
library(DALEX)
library(mlr3)
library(mlr3learners)
library(shapviz)
library(tidyverse)
library(xgboost)
set.seed(479)
theme_set(theme_classic())
```

# Problem 1 [Tree Ensemble Short Answer]

a. Imagine working on a large dataset at a company strong parallel computing
infrastructure. Can the trees $f^{b}$ in boosting be trained in parallel? What
about bagging?

b. As you increase the number of trees $B$, do you expect the model to overfit,
underfit, or "it depends"? Answer for both boosting and bagging.

# Problem 2 [Tree Ensemble Variable Importance]

You are working with a colleague on a classification problem using boosting.
This is the data:

```{r}
#| label: boosting-show
df <- read_csv("https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/raw/refs/heads/master/data/boosting_data.csv")
ix <- sample(nrow(df), 1600)
train <- df[ix, ]
test <- df[-ix, ]
```

You run an initial experiment on the dataset below and find these
variable importances.

```{r boosting-full}
#| fig-width: 4
#| fig-height: 2
task <- as_task_classif(train, target = "y")
learner <- lrn("classif.xgboost", nrounds = 100)
learner$train(task)
xgb.importance(model = learner$model) |>
    ggplot(aes(Gain, reorder(Feature, Gain))) +
    geom_col() +
    labs(x = "Gain", y = "Importance")
```

Your colleague runs the same experiment, but accidentally omitted `x1`, the
variable with the highest importance. You compare the test classification
error for both models.

```{r boosting-reduced}
task_colleague <- as_task_classif(select(train, -x1), target = "y")
learner_colleague <- lrn("classif.xgboost", nrounds = 100)
learner_colleague$train(task_colleague)
tibble(
    model = c("full", "colleague"),
    classif.ce = c(
        learner$predict_newdata(test)$score(msr("classif.ce")),
        learner_colleague$predict_newdata(select(test, -x1))$score(msr("classif.ce"))
    )
)
```

Surprisingly, the performance has gone up! What do you think is going on, and
what are the interpretability implications?

# Problem 3 [LinearSHAP Derivation]

For certain models $f$ and choices of $v$, SHAP values have closed-form expressions. Suppose $f$ is linear and $v$ uses baseline feature removal:
$$f(x) = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^J \beta_j x_j, \qquad v(S) = f(x_S, x'_{\bar{S}})$$

a. Express $v(S)$ in terms of the $\beta_j$, the observed features $x_j$, and
the baseline features $x_j'$.

b. Use your answer from (a) to find an expression for the marginal contribution
$C(j \mid S)$.

c. Show that $\phi_j(f, x) = \beta_j(x_j - x_j')$.

d. Interpret this result. How does the attribution depend on the baseline? Why
might some observations $x$ have larger attributions for feature $j$ than
others?

# Problem 4 [Bikesharing Demand]

The bike demand dataset records hourly rentals along with weather, time-of-day,
and seasonality features. The goal here is to predict hourly demand (`cnt`) and
interpret the resulting models.

```{r bike-setup}
f <- tempfile()
download.file("https://github.com/krisrs1128/treelapse/raw/master/data/bike.rda", f)
bike <- get(load(f)) |>
select(-dteday) |>
    mutate(
    across(where(is.character), as.factor)
)
```

a. Setup a boosting model pipeline that supports tuning over `nrounds`,
`max_depth`, and `eta`. Use the ranges: `nrounds` $\in \left[50, 500\right]$,
`max_depth` $\in \left[1, 6 \right]$, and `eta` $\in \left[0.01, 0.3\right]$.
Report the best configuration and its cross-validated RMSE. *(Note: Because these data were gathered over time, temporal cross-validation would be more accurate, but we're setting that aside since it's outside the scope of the
class.)*

b. Retrain using the full data and the selected hyperparameters. Plot predicted
vs. actual demand. Comment on any systematic patterns you notice.

c. Has the model captured a temperature effect (`temp`)? An interaction between
`hr` and `weekend`? Answer both questions using CP profiles or SHAP
attributions. Support your answer with 1 - 2 figures.

d. Fit an alternative model (e.g., lasso, CART, or bagging) to the same training
data. You do not need to tune hyperparameters, but compute either CP profiles or
SHAP attributions on your fit. Where do the models agree/disagree?

# Problem 5 [CP vs. SHAP Visualizations]

Both CP profiles and SHAP dependence plots show how a single feature relates to
model predictions, but using different mathematical machinery. This problem
compares the approaches using the [census income
dataset](https://archive.ics.uci.edu/dataset/2/adult). As in our notes, we'll
train a boosting classifier to predict whether a person earns more than
\$50K/year.

```{r census-setup}
census <- read_csv("https://github.com/krisrs1128/stat479_notes/raw/refs/heads/master/data/census_processed.csv")
task <- as_task_classif(census, target = "income")
task$positive <- ">50K"

learner <- lrn("classif.xgboost",
    nrounds = 200, eta = 0.05, max_depth = 3, predict_type = "prob"
)
learner$train(task)
```

The figures below show CP profiles and a SHAP dependence plot for `age`.

```{r census-cp}
#| fig-width: 5
#| fig-height: 3
#| out-width: 60%
#| echo: false
explainer <- DALEX::explain(
    learner,
    data = census,
    y = as.integer(census$income == ">50K"),
    predict_function = \(model, newdata) model$predict_newdata(newdata)$prob[, 1],
    verbose = FALSE
)
cp <- predict_profile(explainer, slice_sample(census, n = 100), variables = "age")
plot(cp, variables = "age")
```

```{r census-shap}
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 4
#| out-width: 60%
#| echo: false
X_mat <- census |> select(-income) |> as.matrix()
shp <- shapviz(learner$model, X_pred = X_mat)
sv_dependence(shp, "age", color_var = c())
```

a. In the CP plot, what quantity does each curve trace out as `age` varies? In
the SHAP plot, what does each point represent? Answer using notation from the
lecture notes.

b. How does the efficiency axiom constrain the attributions you see in the SHAP
dependence plot? Compared to the CP plot, does this change how you would explain
the age-income effect to a non-statistical stakeholder?

c. Using either CP or SHAP, produce one additional interpretability plot for
this model. It shoudn't just be the same code applied to a new feature. Include
the figure and briefly discuss its strengths and weaknesses relative to the two
plots above.
